sheets.title: Toldot Trans:  Tracing a Trans Lineage through the Torah
sheets.summary:
A source sheet tracing the lineage of transness from our plural and all-encompassing God, through Adam the bi-gendered being, all the way to Rabbi Yochanan the Transfemme, utilizing rabbinic sources, exegetical techniques and faith. 

The working definition of transness for this source sheet is that a biblical character has "some kind of gender/sex f*ckery going on." These countless examples, in an unbroken chain across millenia, fly in the face of the idea that our "patriarchs" and "matriarchs" model the simple and clear line between "men" and "women" ordained by our "male" God. 

This is just a taste of the silhouette of queerness and transness in our tradition - a teaser-trailer to whet a pallette, to give the first hint of roots and strength to my trans sisters, brothers and siblings, and to inspire us to find more. To claim ourselves neither outside the tradition, nor simply included as an afterthought -- but central to the entire faith.

May the children of Yisrael and Ivrim - the strugglers and crossers - continue to find God in the transition in-between. And may the laugh of Sarah, released in joy when she was told of the transfemininity of Isaac's soul, continue to carry forth, and take form, in our trans children today. May transness always be known as laughter-made-flesh. May our name be an everlasting one, that shall not perish. And may all those in God's image - the sons, the daughters, and those beyond them - find true safety, community, and love.

sheets.title: The Impossible Task of Forgiveness
sheets.summary:
This shiur explores the process of the Ten Days of Teshuva/Return from the lens of an idea called, "Sweetening of the Judgment." There is a "built in" process in the fabric of creation and also expressed in our interpersonal relationships that goes as follows: 
A. Judgment- the holding on of our own truth
B. The addition of mercy and forgiveness when that hinders the relationship
C. Only to discover, that by letting go of "MY truth," I can discover a greater truth and vision hitherto unrevealed to me.

sheets.title: The Power of Hanukkah's Light
sheets.summary: Lighting the Chanukah menorah reflects a move from darkness to light, an embrace of hope for greater light even as the days grow short and the nights grow dark.  Our traditions teach that the cycles of the moon and sun are inherent in Chanukah, but this holiday reinforces the message of Judaism that light is emblematic of understanding (enlightenment), resolve, hope and strength.

sheets.title: Becoming Text People
sheets.summary:
A study sheet for a workshop called "Whatever Comes Up is Jewish: Meeting Our Students Where They Are." You have your deeply Jewish lesson ready to go and then: “My dog died yesterday.” “I caught a spider this morning!” “And after two years he just. . . dumped me!” “My sister is at a protest right now.” They could be kindergartners or 4th graders. They could be 11th graders. Our students arrive in our classes and bring with them everything they are and everything they are thinking about. Our task is to meet them exactly where they are and figure out how to connect them with Judaism, how to engage and strengthen their sense of Jewish identity, and how to help them build community. The good news is, we also arrive in our classes bringing with us everything we are and everything we are thinking about. Rabbi Abraham Joshua Heschel famously taught that we don’t need more text books, but rather more text people. That’s us.

Well, HOW do we become Text People? 
1. Learn. Don't Stop. Credit your teachers.
2. Tell. Create. Embody.
3. Know your core Jewish values.
4. Know your core Jewish messages.
5. Think expansively.

sheets.title: We Are Created Creative
sheets.summary: In the first chapter of Genesis, we are taught that we are all created in the image and likeness of the Divine. For Rabbi Adina Allen, this is point in our story where the innate creativity of each of us is born and resides. She asks the question "...what is starting with God's creation of the universe is less about asserting God's power, and more about inviting us to access and active our own creative potential?" (104) In this chapter of 'The Place of All Possibility,' Rabbi Allen invites us to ponder what does it mean to be created in the image of God, and how can we use that as fuel for our inherent creativity. Join the conversation at adina-allen.com!

sheets.title: Creation Comes from Chaos and Void
sheets.summary: Life is messy, and finding time to tap into our innate creativity can be challenging. But, it inside of the chaos of our lives that Rabbi Adina Allen invites us to explore how the 'perfect' or 'right' moment to create naturally is inside of the chaos, just as it was at moment of chaos through which God created the earth. And, just like God used the primordial elements available in the chaos and void, Rabbi Adina invites us to consider that creativity and the processes of making invites us to use what we have, at any given moment. She tells us, "to be in touch with creativity is to see the potential in everything..." including our mess. Consider this your invitation, adina-alien.com.

Is the Earth a rotating round ball (globe) or stationary (non-moving) flat circular disc with end points?

Is there a dome enclosure of some sort around the Earth?

-The letter that is a square closed on every side but the way creation flowS-

Though it is true that no one can surf the surface of surfaces to see before creation - yet perhaps there are deeper allusions among the dangers of causing damage to the text to project your own illusion upon it. 

this is a thought experiment just to help guide you through some of the features of sefaria. there is no pressure to rush any of it, for the nature of the torah, the words of our sages of blessed memory, those who still may live for 120 or even 180, may the supernal will it; that a single word can provide a lifetime of every kind of jewel and every kind of wonder. too much sweetness makes one sick. it is better to study and delight at the most leisurely place and know each thing you encounter than to act with too much hunger to regurgitate that there was no chance for rumination and all the produce has been stripped from the stalks in plague.

en.prompt: The initial act of creation by God is a foundational moment in the Torah, setting the stage for the entire narrative of the universe and humanity. The opening verses of Genesis describe the moment when God began to create heaven and earth, establishing the divine origin of all things.

en.title: The Beginning
en.prompt: Parashat Bereshit is the first portion of the Torah. It begins with an account of God’s creation of the world.

description.en: Bereshit ("In the Beginning”) is the first parashah in the annual Torah reading cycle, which restarts at the beginning of the book of Genesis. The reading begins with God’s creation of the world. The first people, Adam and Eve, eat from the Tree of Knowledge and are then banished from the Garden of Eden. Their elder son, Cain, kills their younger son, Abel, and Cain is destined to a life of wandering.
description.he: פרשת בראשית, הפרשה הראשונה בתורה, מתארת את ראשיתו של העולם. אלוהים בורא את העולם בשישה ימים ונח ביום השביעי. הוא מניח את האדם בגן עדן ומצווה עליו ליהנות מפירות הגן מלבד מפרי עץ הדעת. בעצת הנחש חוה ואדם אוכלים מעץ הדעת, ולכן מגורשים מגן עדן. בנם הבכור קין הורג את אחיו הצעיר הבל, וקין נידון להיות נע ונד בארץ.

en.title: The Beginning of Creation
en.prompt: The opening of Genesis marks the very beginning of creation, where God initiates the creation of heaven and earth. The Book of Genesis introduces the creation narrative, detailing how God created the universe, including the heavens and the earth.

description.en: Stories of the world's creation appears in the first two chapters of the book of Genesis. Generations of Jewish thinkers have spilled much ink analyzing the meaning and significance of these chapters.

description.he: השמיים נזכרים בפסוקים הראשונים של ספר בראשית, ובמקורות הם לרוב מייצגים את כל מה שנמצא מעל כדור הארץ. כמו כן השמיים נתפסים כמקום שהאל והמלאכים שוכנים בו.
description.en: The Heavens (Shamayim) appear in the first lines of Genesis and are mentioned frequently in the Jewish sources as representing everything above Earth. The Heavens are also understood as the place where God and the angels reside.
